title: The Almanack
subtitle: A compendium of life pro tips for becoming a mature, financially sound, and spatially aware man — early.

tips.title: Marry early, make kids early
tips.content: There's a compounding effect to starting a family early that nobody talks about. When you marry and have kids in your mid-twenties, you grow up alongside your children. By the time they're teenagers, you're still young enough to be active, relatable, and financially hitting your stride. By 50, your kids are independent adults — and you still have decades of energy and freedom ahead. The math works: early commitment compounds into long-term freedom. Most people optimize for freedom in their twenties and end up scrambling for stability in their forties. Flip the script.

tips.title: Standardize your breakfast — eliminate daily decision fatigue
tips.content:
The single best health hack is removing breakfast from your decision-making entirely. Pick one nutritionally complete meal, repeat it every day, and automate the grocery ordering around it.
The recipe: Overnight steel-cut or rolled oats, 2 dates, 2–3 Brazil nuts (selenium), 4–5 almonds (vitamin E, magnesium), 2 walnuts (omega-3s), a rotation of pumpkin seeds (zinc), ½ tbsp fenugreek powder (blood sugar regulation), and ½ tbsp each of chia seeds (fiber, omega-3s) and poppy seeds (minerals). Soak everything in milk the night before and leave it on the counter — not the fridge. Cold temps prevent oats from absorbing properly.
In the morning: microwave 30 seconds to 1 minute, stir in a tablespoon of chunky peanut butter (protein + healthy fats), and top with thin slices of half a banana (potassium, natural sweetness). Have your coffee after eating, not before — caffeine on an empty stomach spikes cortisol and tanks your energy by noon.
Why this works: every ingredient earns its spot nutritionally. Brazil nuts cover your daily selenium in 2–3 pieces. Fenugreek stabilizes blood sugar so you don't crash at 10am. Chia seeds expand in liquid and keep you full for hours. The peanut butter adds enough protein and fat to make it a real meal, not just carbs. And the banana is the only sugar source — no honey, no maple syrup, no granola.
The real point isn't the recipe. It's the system. When breakfast is decided, your grocery list is decided, your morning routine is decided, and you start every day with one fewer thing to think about. That compounds over years.
